In the beginning, the sky was a heavy blanket of indigo wool, woven so tightly by the Spider Spirits that no light could pass through. The animals of the earth stumbled in darkness, bruising their knees on rocks and shivering in the cold. 

A small, audacious hummingbird named Zale dared to fly higher than the rest. While the eagles and hawks feared the Spider Spirits, Zale’s wings beat so fast they became invisible. He ascended until the air grew thin and his tiny heart hammered against his ribs. There, he found the seam of the indigo blanket.

With his needle-sharp beak, Zale pecked at a loose thread. He pulled and pulled, unraveling a single stitch. A blinding beam of gold—the sun—pierced through, warming his feathers. Emboldened, he pecked again, creating a smaller hole that let through the silver glow of the moon.

The Spider Spirits, awakened by the light, chased him away, but the damage was done. As Zale fled, his frantic wings poked thousands of tiny holes in the fabric of the night. Today, we call the large tear the Sun, the smaller one the Moon, and the tiny punctures the Stars, reminding us that even the smallest creature can bring light to the world.

Here is a short myth about the origin of the echo.

Long ago, before the mountains had names, a young spirit named Vako lived in the canyons. He possessed a voice of pure gold, capable of mimicking the roar of a lion or the whisper of the wind through dry grass. Vako was proud, believing his voice was the only one worthy of being heard. He would interrupt the birds' morning songs and shout over the thunder, demanding the world listen only to him.

The Sky Goddess, weary of his arrogance, descended upon a cloud of silent mist. "You love your voice so dearly," she spoke, her tone like falling rain, "that you shall keep it forever, but you shall never again have a thought of your own to speak."

She touched his throat with a sliver of moonlight. Vako opened his mouth to protest, but no words came. Panic seized him until a hawk screeched nearby. Instantly, Vako’s voice mimicked the screech perfectly. He tried to beg for mercy, but could only repeat the last words the Goddess had spoken: "...thought of your own to speak."

Now, Vako wanders the lonely places of the world, hiding in caves and valleys. He waits desperately for someone to speak, just so he can use his golden voice one more time, fading slowly into the stone as nothing but an echo.
